item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first image could not be sourced reliably; however, similar images of the same object (that being the DNEPR rocket that carried the RASAT satellite) indicate that the image was taken by Surrey Satellite Technology Ltd. Due to the lack of an explicit Creative Commons License, it is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0.

The second image, according to this 2004 article, belongs to here. Due to the lack of an explicit Creative Commons License, it is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0.

Both images have since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Turkey Holocaust.
Giraffe Hell.
The Goose Terminator.
Dial-a-llama.

I'm sure I'm missing some, but we seem to have developed a sub-genre of species-specific targeted anomalies.
Which to me implies that the scp-verse has multiple levels of reality, each dedicated to the mythology and urban legends of different animals. Much like the majority of the mainlist is based on human zeitgeist. With only a handful of the most obvious examples getting noticed by The Foundation with their human-centric cultural biases.

This headcanon makes me grin, and I refuse to recant it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At least Mr Gobbles lived up to his namesake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor Mr Gobbles…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well. Herons are dicks.

And this is actually really cool, considering I skim over an article before I full read it, the hook here made the name make sense and left a good hole of wonder as to why in the first place, and why it stopped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the whole bit about Turkey and Turkish seems a bit too much on the nose (I do realize there's a bit of historical connection between the words in English), but this is still a pretty damn fun article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Guineafowl and Turkeys aren't the same thing. The American bird was basically a misnomer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While 20cm x 20cm x 20cm is better described as a cube, if you know of somewhere to get two-dimensional objects - well, I for one am very afraid.

…I deserved that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's a place turkeys go to escape the November slaughter?….

…oh.

Well now I'm sad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to hate this, but I can't stay mad. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While 20cm x 20cm x 20cm is better described as a cube, if you know of somewhere to get two-dimensional objects - well, I for one am very afraid.

Or, with less snark: all plates are three-dimensional. Usually the thickness is much less than the width and depth, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, crap, I just realized:

The instance contained a 20cm x 20cm x 20cm steel plate

Who the hell makes three-dimensional plates?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alas, Mr. Gobbles, we hardly knew ye…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well that escalated quickly

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anyone else think this wouldn't be out of place in an Aqua Teen Hunger Force episode?

-Edit- Whoops, didn't mean to hit reply…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I AM THE GREAT TURKEY!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Which in itself was derived from "Hindi", since a similar bird that was sold to the Turks from India.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Turkish doesn't go back further than the 11th century, when the Seljuks conquered Anatolia. Maybe use "archaic Turkish" instead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh…for some reasons, it doesn't let me change the second picture with the new format. Can someone do it, please?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's with Mr. Gobbles?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fantastic but…

Mr. Gobbles…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, yeah, I know I am late, but I wanted to thank Accelerando for helping me with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun fact: "Hindler" is a deviation of "hindiler", which is "turkeys" in Turkish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps this breakdown in the laws of nature was caused by human thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why does a breakdown in the laws of nature care about the fact that, in English, the bird and the nation are pronounced the same?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Verging on necropost here, but it feels rude to ignore a direct question.

The herons are great, but their chosen enemy (non-anomalous Earth turkeys like Mr Gobbles) seems like a silly choice, considering that, y'know, humans are here, and are arguably the more advanced civilization.

This isn't a problem with the article! Just funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incredible, in the legal sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…I can't believe I missed this. Hindler.

Heil Hindler!

Scenes of desperation

Dear god, the herons…they're goose-stepping into Paris! Who can stop that fowl Hindler!?

-hushed tones-

We must send Him in.

You don't mean..

Yes. We rebuilt him. Made him better, faster, stronger than ever before. He will take the fight straight to Hindler's coop. God-speed, Captain Fowl...

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I assume herons seem too silly, but i've seen pictures of herons literally swallowing baby bunnies whole, so that can't be it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no hard-and-fast rule about how to do subobject notation, but it doesn't make a lot of sense any other way. It's hard to believe that the Foundation wouldn't use a nomenclature that has an easy to understand term for "all of the items that comprise this SCP, together".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Knew it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can, there's no rule saying that you can't choose your own sub-designation. I've also gone ahead and localized yer images for ya :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, my bad. I always thought the author could choose sub-object designation.

Also, I don't really know how to do it without screwing the format, that's why I hotlink'd them…

E: I suppose it's better now.

E2: Thanks Roget for fixing the pictures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a bit of subobject notation fail here. The alternative universe should be -1, the vehicles should be -2, and the Great Turkey should be -3; SCP-1827 should refer to the entire aggregate SCP, not just one component.

Edit: Also, please upload the images to this file instead of hotlinking them to the sandbox.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To answer your questions:

1- Yes. However, they used real turkeys for the pictures.

2- Yes. And the herons won.

3- Something is weakening the herons. Guess who? The turkeys.

By the way, why are the herons a terrible choice of enemies?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, he's very convincing. I figured that the third note was from a heron, and the second note as well, but I legitimately thought the first one was an actual Great Turkey.

Oh, right. In retrospect, the "hindler is real" bit should have given it away. Guess I'm super dumb!

Now I have a lot of questions. Was everything sent out in the 1827-1 instances fake, to simulate a turkey world and lure turkeys in? Was the Turkey-Heron War mentioned in the romance novel real? Why do the notes become less convincing each time?

Despite my confusion, I still really like this! The herons have all the makings of a terrifying and fascinating alien threat, except for one thing: terrible choice of enemies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He is actually an heron faking to be a turkey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Done, thanks. There are a couple of other dodgy sentences, but I didn't want to change the wording too much, so I left them as-is for now.

Suggested changes.
I put maybe too much thought into this.

"photographic and shootage records"
"shootage" apparently isn't a word, you could use "photographic and video records"

"The actual texts of the songs"
This is just my opinion, but "lyrics" or "vocals" might work better than "texts"

"before brutally abrupting"
"Abrupting" isn't a word. You could go with "before abruptly terminating", which has the same meaning of a sudden unplanned end to the transmission.

"abrupting, after said creatures proceeded to supposedly kill and devour "Mr. Gobbles"."
The wording here makes it tricky to tell what order things happened in.

If the camera cut out before Mr Gobbles was attacked, it should be "after which said creatures proceeded to supposedly kill and devour Mr Gobbles." But then, how did the Foundation know what was going on? (I'm guessing this is why the word "supposedly" is in there, but the uncertainty is still a little weird)

If the camera cut out after Mr Gobbles was attacked, it should be "after said creatures had proceeded to kill and devour Mr Gobbles". But then, the camera cutting out wouldn't really be that abrupt.

My suggestion is to have the camera cut out during the attack. "as the creatures proceeded to kill and devour Mr Gobbles". That way you get the abruptness and the certainty!

The Great Turkey's first note also has some strange wording, but, y'know, he's a turkey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooft, this really needs some proofreading. Tagliaferro, I can roll through and fix up the minor grammatical errors, if you like. Stuff like "capable to access" should be "able to access", or "capable of accessing".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They are not turkeys, dammit. They are herons trying to trick turkeys to think that they are turkeys. But yeah, fuck Thanksgiving.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck Thanksgiving.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happy Thanksgiving, by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ugh, this was tiresome. It's a portal to another world but with turkeys lol.

At least that's what I got out of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay. This is amazing. +1. But make it a -J.

(Major apologies to all the Turks of the world who happen to be reading this. Some English-language puns are just too terrible to pass up. I know this. The author knows this.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, turkeys that come from Turkey? That's a little too silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1827-2 does not appear to be interested to make a contact with the humankind, nor it does appear to be aware of it.

This is a bit redundant. Why would they try to make contact if they're not aware of us?

And now we've got a dimension for turkeys, and a dimension for potatoes. Now all we need is a dimension for pumpkin pie. (Don't care still love this +1)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't decide if you should go on to make all the SCPs or never be allowed to write anything ever again. There has to be some kind of memetic effect doesn't it? Because there is no good reason this should work. At all. This is weirder than the goose-murder-bot. …

What the fuck is with these weird, strangely-specifically-avian SCPs lately?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what

what the hell is this

+1
